---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slidegreen

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size60[
Add confounders to our research project dataset
]

<br>
<br>
<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Fall 2021)
]]



---

class: slidepurple

.size60[**Wheelan chapter 7: Data**]

.pull-left[.size40[
+ Selection bias

+ Publication bias

+ Recall bias

+ Survivorship bias

+ Healthy user bias
]]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', out.width='65%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/10_2-Wheelan_Cover.jpg")
```
]

???

CLASS: What questions do you have about the data issues raised by the Wheelan chapter?

Chapter 7 The Importance of Data (110-126)

Garbage in, garbage out; 
Three things we all of data, 
1. A sample to represent the population, 
2. provide comparisons between treatment and control groups, 
3. Just because (data that can be mined); 

Types of data (longitudinal aka across time cross sectional aka at one point in time); 

examples of garbage data: 

selection bias: How have we chosen the sample? Random or not?

publication bias: Positive findings more likely to be published. The null results go in a drawer.

recall bias: Memory is a deeply problematic thing. Especially as time passes.

survivorship bias: Did the sample change over time in a way that messes up our conclusions? If we lose the failing cases the remaining will look even better!

healthy user bias: Unidentified confounder problem. Do vitamins work? Maybe but you have to deal with the fact that healthy, wealthy people are more likely to take them!







---

.size40[.center[**Do countries with larger military enlistments have fewer political freedoms?**]]

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.height = 3, fig.width=5, out.width='70%'}
## Manual DAG
tibble(
  x = c(-3, 3, 0),
  y = c(1, 1, 2),
  labels = c("Military\n Enlistment", "Political\n Freedoms", "???")
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 8) +
  theme_void() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-4, 4), ylim = c(0.8, 2.1)) +
  geom_label(aes(label = labels), size = 7) +
    annotate("segment", x = .4, xend = 2.5, y = 1.9, yend = 1.2, arrow = arrow()) +
  annotate("segment", x = -.4, xend = -2.5, y = 1.9, yend = 1.2, arrow = arrow()) +
  annotate("segment", x = -1.5, xend = 1.6, y = 1, yend = 1, arrow = arrow())
```

???

CLASS: Let's talk confounders we should consider from the WDI database.  

- What did you find? 



YOU: Make sure to pull multiple variable options in case of data coverage problems

DEMONSTRATE: Pull and clean the data THEN distribute it to the class



---

class: slidegreen

.size80[**For Tuesday**]

<br>

.size50[
Build univariate analyses of the new confounder data on its own

+ Tables, histograms, maps and descriptive statistics
]
